title: FFMI Calculator: Fat-Free Mass Index
tagline: Calculate your Fat-Free Mass Index (FFMI) from weight, height, and body fat percentage to assess muscle development.
intro: Enter your total body weight, height, and body fat percentage to compute FFMI and normalized FFMI — a more meaningful measure of muscularity than BMI.
formula: Formula: FFM = weight × (1 − body fat%) · FFMI = FFM ÷ height² · Normalized FFMI = FFMI + 6.1 × (1.8 − height in m)

error.invalid: Enter valid weight, height, and body fat percentage (0–99%).

category.exceptional: Exceptional — may warrant scrutiny for PED use

about.heading: About the FFMI Calculator
about.body:
The Fat-Free Mass Index (FFMI) is a body composition metric that measures the amount of lean (fat-free) tissue a person carries relative to their height. Unlike the Body Mass Index (BMI), which simply divides total body weight by height squared, FFMI first subtracts estimated body fat to obtain fat-free mass (FFM) and then scales that value by height. The result is a number that reflects muscularity independent of how much fat a person is carrying, making it far more useful for athletes, bodybuilders, and fitness enthusiasts who are deliberately building muscle mass.

The calculation proceeds in three steps. First, fat-free mass is computed by multiplying total body weight by the complement of body fat percentage: FFM (kg) = weight × (1 − bodyFat% / 100). Second, FFMI is obtained by dividing FFM by height in metres squared: FFMI = FFM ÷ height². Third, a normalized FFMI is calculated to standardize comparisons across people of different heights, using the correction factor proposed by Kouri and colleagues in their landmark 1995 study: Normalized FFMI = FFMI + 6.1 × (1.8 − height in metres). This adjustment brings all values to a common reference height of 1.8 m, making it straightforward to compare muscular development between a 1.65 m person and a 1.90 m person.

Interpretation of normalized FFMI follows broadly accepted categories derived from population studies of competitive and recreational athletes. Values below 17.0 are below average, 17.0–18.0 reflect normal muscular development for a reasonably active person, 18.0–20.0 is above average and typical of dedicated gym-goers, 20.0–22.0 is excellent and characterises experienced strength athletes, and 22.0–25.0 is superior, typical of elite natural bodybuilders. The famous Kouri 1995 paper found that pre-steroid-era champion bodybuilders — athletes who competed before anabolic steroids became widely available — clustered below an FFMI of about 25. Values above 25 were rare among verified natural athletes, which is why an FFMI above 25 is sometimes used as a rough screening indicator for possible performance-enhancing drug (PED) use in anti-doping contexts, though this is not a definitive diagnostic criterion.

Body fat percentage measurement accuracy profoundly affects FFMI reliability. Common methods include DEXA (dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry, the gold standard), underwater weighing (hydrostatic densitometry), BodPod (air displacement plethysmography), skinfold callipers, and bioelectrical impedance analysis (BIA). BIA devices, including most consumer smart scales, tend to vary substantially based on hydration status and have meaningful error margins. Skinfold measurements depend heavily on technique. For the most reliable FFMI tracking over time, use a consistent measurement method.

Gender differences in typical FFMI values are substantial. Women naturally carry more essential fat and have lower absolute lean mass than men, so the categories described above apply primarily to males. Female athletes and bodybuilders typically achieve normalized FFMI values several points lower than equivalent male counterparts, and a female FFMI of 20+ would represent an exceptionally high level of muscularity. Research-based normative tables for women are less well-established, so interpret female results with this context in mind.

examples.heading: FFMI calculation examples
examples.intro: Click any example to load typical body composition values into the calculator.

items.note: Superior category — experienced natural strength athlete near the upper range of natural muscularity.
Exceptional category — well above natural upper limit; this level warrants scrutiny for PED use in competitive contexts.
Above-average female category — good muscle tone with healthy body fat.
Superior category — solid body composition for a gym-trained male.

howto.heading: How to use the FFMI Calculator
steps: Measure your body weight accurately and select the matching unit (kg or lbs).
Measure your height and select the matching unit (cm or inches).
Enter your body fat percentage — obtained from DEXA, callipers, BIA scale, or another method.
Click Calculate FFMI to see your fat-free mass, FFMI, normalized FFMI, and category.
Track changes over months to monitor progress — normalized FFMI should rise if you are building muscle while controlling body fat.

items.q: What is a good FFMI score?
items.a: For men, a normalized FFMI of 18–20 is above average for a fit individual, 20–22 is excellent and typical of experienced strength athletes, and 22–25 is superior, representing elite natural bodybuilders. Very few verified natural athletes exceed 25. For women, values run several points lower on average.

items.q: How does FFMI differ from BMI?
items.a: BMI divides total body weight by height squared and cannot distinguish muscle from fat. A muscular athlete with very low body fat may have the same BMI as an overweight sedentary person. FFMI removes the fat component first, giving a much more meaningful measure of lean mass relative to height.

Normalized FFMI applies a height correction so that results are comparable across people of different statures. The formula adds 6.1 × (1.8 − height in metres) to the raw FFMI, adjusting everyone to a reference height of 1.8 m. This prevents taller people from appearing to have lower muscularity simply because of their stature.

items.q: How accurate does my body fat measurement need to be?
items.a: Body fat accuracy is the main source of error in FFMI calculations. A 2–3% error in body fat can shift FFMI by 1–2 points, which can change the category. DEXA is the most accurate consumer-accessible method. If using BIA (smart scales), always measure under the same conditions — morning, fasted, hydrated — to minimize variability.

Yes, though the interpretation categories are primarily derived from male athlete data. Women naturally carry more essential fat and build less absolute lean mass, so a female FFMI above 18 already represents a highly muscular physique. The calculation itself is equally valid for women; only the benchmark categories differ.

items.q: Is FFMI above 25 proof of steroid use?
items.a: No. An FFMI above 25 is a statistical outlier among verified natural athletes and may warrant scrutiny in competitive natural bodybuilding. However, it is not proof of drug use — exceptional genetics, advanced training, and precise nutrition can occasionally produce values in this range naturally. It is a screening signal, not a diagnostic test.
